title: The Quiet Power of Compound Interest Most People Never Grasp
excerpt: Einstein allegedly called it the eighth wonder of the world. But most people treat it as a math problem rather than a life philosophy. Here's why that's a costly mistake.
content:
Compound interest is one of those concepts that everyone learns once in school and then promptly forgets — until they're staring at a retirement calculator wondering where the decades went.

The math isn't complicated. The *psychology* is.

## Why We Underestimate It

Humans are hardwired to think linearly. If something grows 10% each year, our instinct is to multiply 10% by 30 years and assume 300% total growth. But that's not how compounding works.

A ₹10,00,000 investment at 12% annual return:
- After 10 years: ₹31,06,000
- After 20 years: ₹96,46,000
- After 30 years: ₹2,99,60,000

The last 10 years produce more wealth than the first 20 combined.

## The Time Variable Nobody Talks About

Every personal finance book will tell you to start early. Few explain *why* in a way that sticks emotionally.

The answer: time is a multiplier on the multiplier. When you invest at 22 instead of 32, you're not just adding 10 years — you're adding 10 years of exponential acceleration.

## The Behavioral Economics Angle

Working in behavioral science taught me something important: we systematically *discount* the future. This is called hyperbolic discounting — a ₹10,000 reward today feels far more valuable than ₹50,000 in 15 years, even when the math says otherwise.

The antidote is simple: automate. Remove the decision from your hands entirely. A SIP you set up once and forget is more powerful than a thousand 'I'll invest next month' intentions.

> "The stock market is a device for transferring money from the impatient to the patient." — Warren Buffett

## Start Boring, Stay Rich

Stop thinking of compound interest as a financial tool. Think of it as a **patience tax**. The market charges impatience. Every year you wait, you pay a premium — not in rupees lost today, but in future wealth surrendered.

The antidote is deceptively simple: start small, start now, don't stop.

title: I Built a Subscriber Engagement Model at Work. Then I Applied It to My Own Life.
excerpt: The RFV framework — Recency, Frequency, Value — is how we score subscriber engagement at The Ken. Turns out it's a surprisingly sharp lens for personal finance too.
content:
At The Ken, my job involves figuring out which subscribers are going to renew and which are going to churn. We do this with a model called RFV — Recency, Frequency, Value.

- **Recency**: When did they last engage?
- **Frequency**: How often do they come back?
- **Value**: How much are they contributing?

The model is a product analytics tool. But sitting with it for two years, I've started seeing it everywhere — including in my own financial habits.

## The RFV of Your Money

Think about your relationship with your own finances through this lens:

**Recency**: When did you last look at your portfolio? Your expense tracker? If the answer is 'months ago', you're already a low-RFV user of your own financial life.

**Frequency**: How often do you make intentional financial decisions vs. letting inertia decide? Inertia is the default — and in finance, the default is usually suboptimal.

**Value**: What's the actual ROI on your time spent thinking about money? Most people either obsess (checking portfolios daily, panic-selling) or ignore entirely. Neither is high-value behavior.

## What High-RFV Financial Behavior Looks Like

In our subscriber data, the highest-RFV users aren't the ones who read the most articles. They're the ones who read *consistently*, even if infrequently — and who act on what they read.

The financial equivalent: you don't need to be a market-watcher. You need to be a *consistent* actor. One intentional financial decision per week — rebalancing, reviewing, or simply reading one good thing — compounds over time in ways that sporadic bursts never do.

The insight from product analytics: **engagement is a habit, not an event.**

title: Your Emergency Fund Is a Mental Health Investment
excerpt: Most financial advice treats the emergency fund as purely mathematical. But the real value isn't the six months of expenses — it's the psychological freedom that comes with it.

title: What Playing Semi-Pro Football Taught Me About Data
excerpt: I played in the Karnataka C-division league this year for Triveni FC. It turns out 90 minutes of competitive football teaches you things about performance metrics that no dashboard ever will.
content:
I spent most of 2025 as a product analyst by day and a midfielder by evening — playing in the Karnataka state C-division league for Triveni FC.

Playing semi-professionally after years away from competitive football forced me to think about performance in a way that no product dashboard ever has.

## The Problem With Vanity Metrics

In football, there are metrics that *look* good and metrics that *win* games. Passes completed is a vanity metric if 80% of them are sideways balls under no pressure. Expected goals (xG) is closer to the truth — but even then, you can have a high xG game and lose because your defensive shape was terrible.

I see the same thing in product analytics every day. Pageviews. Session time. Open rates. These are the passes-completed of product metrics — directionally useful, dangerously incomplete.

## The Real Signal: Pressure Decisions

What separates good midfielders from great ones isn't what they do with easy balls. It's the quality of decisions under pressure — when there's a defender closing in, when time is short, when the safe option is a 10-yard pass backwards.

At work, the equivalent is what your product does when users are confused, frustrated, or about to churn. That's where the real signal lives.

## Effort Is Not a Metric

The hardest lesson from semi-pro football: effort is not a proxy for performance. I could run 12km in a match and still be a net negative if I was making bad positional decisions.

Most companies measure effort in disguise — hours worked, lines of code, number of features shipped. The uncomfortable question is always: **did it make the product better for the user?**

Football has a built-in answer to this question: the scoreboard. Product teams should be so lucky.

title: How Moneyball Went from Revolution to Table Stakes
excerpt: In 2002, the Oakland A's used statistics to challenge baseball orthodoxy. Twenty years later, every team has an analytics department. What happens when the edge becomes standard?

title: Getting to 1700 ELO: What Chess Taught Me About Decision-Making Under Uncertainty
excerpt: I reached 1700 ELO on rapid chess — top 1% globally. The path there involved failing a lot, pattern-matching even more, and discovering that chess is mostly a game of not-losing rather than winning.

title: Re-reading Kahneman Five Years Later: What Actually Stuck
excerpt: Thinking, Fast and Slow is one of those books everyone references and few actually remember. I re-read it five years after the first time — and the chapter that hit hardest wasn't the one everyone quotes.
content:
I first read Kahneman's masterwork in 2019 during my fellowship at Ashoka — underlined half of it, wrote margins full of exclamation marks, and then proceeded to exhibit every cognitive bias the book warns against.

So I read it again. With fresh eyes and two years of behavioral science fieldwork behind me.

## What Everyone Remembers

System 1 vs System 2 thinking. The fast, intuitive brain vs the slow, deliberate one. It's the elevator pitch that launched a thousand TED talks and twice as many consultant decks.

It's also a simplification. Kahneman himself has expressed reservations about how popularly the framework was adopted.

## What I Actually Use From the Book

Working in behavioral science at Worxogo meant designing nudges — small interventions that push people toward better decisions without restricting choice. Kahneman's work on **loss aversion** was foundational here.

People feel losses roughly 2x more intensely than equivalent gains. This isn't a quirk — it's load-bearing in how you design any incentive system. Framing a sales nudge as 'you're 3 deals behind your target' outperforms 'you're 3 deals away from a bonus', even when the math is identical.

I used this daily. It never got old.

## The Chapter Nobody Talks About

'Experienced Well-being vs. Remembered Well-being.'

Kahneman's research on this distinction is quietly one of the most unsettling things I've read. We don't evaluate experiences as they happen — we evaluate them based on how they peak and how they end.

A vacation can be wonderful for two weeks and be remembered as bad if the last day was terrible. A painful procedure is remembered as less painful if it ended gradually rather than abruptly.

This means we are **systematically bad at making choices that maximize actual happiness** — because we're optimizing for memory, not experience.

## The Unanswered Question

If our remembering self and experiencing self are different entities with different preferences, which one should we optimize for?

Kahneman doesn't fully answer this. I'm not sure anyone can.

But sitting with the question has changed how I think about decisions, travel, conversations, and what I measure as 'a good day.'

**Rating: 5/5** — Essential, and worth a second read.

title: My 2024 Reading List: 18 Books, Ranked Honestly
excerpt: I read 18 books in 2024. Some were transformative, some were disappointing, and one confirmed a theory I'd had for years about how behavioral science gets misapplied in the real world.

title: In Defense of Reading Slowly in an Age of Summaries
excerpt: We live in the age of Blinkist and 'book in 15 minutes' videos. I think they're mostly a trap — and here's why slowing down is the actual productivity move.
